rulings.Case Title: FREDERICK HENRY HOWARD JR VS. UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Vacate Arbitration Award
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion calendar for Monday, August 5, 2024, Line 1. 3 - PLAINTIFF FREDERICK HOWARD's Motion To Vacate Arbitration Award. Plaintiff Frederick Howard's motion to vacate the arbitration award is denied as untimely. Howard and defendants Uber and its affiliates (Uber) arbitrated at JAMS in San Francisco after this court granted a petition to compel arbitration in May 2019. After discovery, briefing, and a hearing, the arbitrator issued an award dismissing Howard's claims. Howard now files this motion to vacate the arbitration award, on the grounds that the arbitrator exceeded their powers, and that Howard was prejudiced by the arbitrator's refusal to consider evidence that Uber failed to provide. Howard accedes to the fact that he received JAMS notifications by email and downloaded copies of the final arbitration award. (Reply Declaration of Frederick Howard, 2-3.) The arbitrator issued an order that JAMS Streamlined Arbitration Rules & Procedures applied to the case, which states that "[d]elivery of e-service documents through the JAMS Electronic Filing System shall be considered as valid and effective service and shall have the same legal effect as an original paper document." (Surreply Declaration of Jared Greenberg, Exh. C-D.) Service of the final award was served on the parties on January 25, 2024. (Id., Exh. E.) Howard filed his motion to vacate on June 17, 2024, 144 days later. As CCP 1288 mandates that a "petition to vacate an award or to correct an award shall be served and filed not later than 100 days after the date of the service of a signed copy of the award on the petitioner," Howard's motion is untimely. (CCP 1288.2.) Howard has not pointed to any reason (other than his status as a pro per litigant) the court should equitably toll the statutory deadline. (Law Finance Group, LLC v. Key (2023) 14 Cal.5th 932, 956, holding that CCP 1288.2 is subject to both equitable tolling and equitable estoppel.) Self-represented "litigants must follow correct rules of procedure." (ViaView, Inc. v. Retzlaff (2016) 1 Cal.App.5th 198, 208.) For the 9:30 a.m. Law & Motion calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 302 remotely. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(302/RBU)

To Provide Verifications To Request For Production Of Documents, Set One. The judge pro tem's report and recommendation is adopted. For the 9:30 a.m. Law & Motion calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 302 remotely. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and attaches a copy of the report and recommendation and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(302/RBU)

Pro Tem Judge David McDonald, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. Counsel should be prepared to discuss, along with the issues raised in the motions, whether a discovery referee should be appointed per CCP 638 or 639. The Court is cognizant of the upcoming MSJ and trial deadlines, however, the level of contentiousness between the parties and counsel appears to require judicial management of these disputes on a heightened level beyond what can be efficiently handled by the Discovery and Law and Motion departments. For the 9:00 a.m. Discovery calendar, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link (DISCOVERY, DEPARTMENT 525 DAILY AT 9:00 A.M.), or dial the corresponding number and use the meeting ID, and password for Discovery Department 525. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to davididaho14@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must e-mail it to the Judge Pro Tem.
